entry.title: Beyond Basic AI Error Handling in SAP CPI - A Rules-First Architecture with HANA Cloud Learning
entry.content:
The Problem Every Integration Developer Knows

Every SAP CPI developer has lived this scenario.

An iFlow fails in production at an inconvenient hour. You open the MPL log, stare at a cryptic stack trace, and spend the next 30–45 minutes manually cross-referencing adapter configurations, checking credentials, and testing payloads. By the time you identify the root cause  a null field in a mapping, an expired OAuth token, a malformed XML payload  the fix takes five minutes. The diagnosis took an hour.

Recent SAP Community posts have explored using the AI Adapter to automate this diagnosis, and the concept is genuinely sound. But the most common approach  sending the raw exception directly to an LLM has five problems that make it unsuitable for real production environments:

ProblemReal Impact
Raw payload sent to LLMBusiness data leaves the tenant — GDPR risk
Every error hits the LLMAuth failures and timeouts: unnecessary API cost at scale
No deduplicationSame error 1000 times = 1000 API calls
Free-text responseCannot be routed, filtered, or acted on programmatically
Response goes back to senderDevelopers never see the diagnosis

This post describes a different architecture — one that classifies errors deterministically first, sanitizes all business data before any AI call, caches intelligence in SAP HANA Cloud, and uses AI only for errors that are genuinely novel.

In my testing: 3 of 4 error types never touched the LLM at all.

What I Built: The ErrorAnalyzer Agent

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent is a native SAP CPI package  two iFlows, 13 Groovy scripts, a HANA Cloud free-tier instance, and the SAP AI Adapter configured with a breakthrough setting that is completely undocumented in SAP's official documentation.

Package contents:

  • EA_TestHarness — the main analyser iFlow, triggered via ProcessDirect
  • EA_VictimFlow — an example source iFlow that deliberately triggers errors to demonstrate the full pipeline

Technologies used:

  • SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)  iFlows, Groovy scripts, JDBC adapter, AI Adapter, Mail adapter
  • SAP HANA Cloud  free tier, with NLP vector engine enabled for V2
  • OpenRouter  LLM gateway (free tier models available)
  • OpenAI  via OpenRouter for AI diagnosis

Architecture Overview

The diagram below shows the complete three-zone architecture. Zone 1 handles detection inside SAP CPI. Zone 2 contains the intelligence layer — classification, sanitization, and dedup logic. Zone 3 is the AI and persistence layer.

 

 

The key design principle: rules first, AI only when necessary. Known errors (auth failures, timeouts, malformed payloads) are handled deterministically in milliseconds with zero LLM cost. Only genuinely novel or complex errors reach Claude.

Prerequisites

Before building, ensure you have the following:

  • SAP Integration Suite trial or production tenant with Cloud Integration capability enabled
  • OpenRouter account  free tier available at openrouter.ai (no credit card required for free models)
  • SAP HANA Cloud free tier instance provisioned on BTP (covered in setup steps below)
  • Gmail or SMTP account for email notifications
  • Postman or any REST client for testing

Step 1 — Security Material Setup

Before configuring any adapter, create two security artifacts in CPI Monitor.

1a. OpenRouter API Key

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Field Value

NameOpenRouter_API_Key
TypeSecure Parameter
ValueBearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxxxx    ← include "Bearer " prefix — this is critical

Click Deploy. Wait for status to show Deployed  before proceeding.

Important: Do not store only the API key. Store the complete value including the "Bearer " prefix with a space. The AI Adapter uses this as the entire Authorization header value. This is part of the undocumented configuration covered in detail in Step 4.

 

1b. HANA Cloud User Credentials

Navigate to: Monitor → Security Material → Create → User Credentials

Field Value

NameHANA_CLOUD_DB_USER
TypeUser Credentials
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour HANA instance DBADMIN password

Step 2 — HANA Cloud Setup

2a. Provision the HANA Cloud Instance

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → SAP HANA Cloud → select hana-free plan. Open HANA Cloud Central → Create Instance → SAP HANA Database. Enable the Natural Language Processing (NLP) feature in Advanced Settings  this activates the vector engine for V2 semantic search.

Free tier note: The instance stops every evening automatically and is deleted after 30 days of inactivity. Restart it manually when needed from HANA Cloud Central.

2b. Configure JDBC Data Source in CPI

Navigate to: Monitor → Manage JDBC Material → JDBC Data Source tab → Add

Field Value

NameHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Database TypeSAP HANA Cloud
JDBC URLjdbc:sap://<your-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com:443?encrypt=true&validateCertificate=true
UserDBADMIN
PasswordYour DBADMIN password
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

Click Deploy → Test Connection. You should see a green success confirmation before proceeding.

 

2c. Create the Database Tables

Open HANA Cloud Central → SQL Console → connect using DBADMIN credentials → run the following SQL:

CREATE TABLE ERROR_PATTERNS (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    EXCEPTION_CLASS  NVARCHAR(255),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    ENVIRONMENT      NVARCHAR(20),
    AI_SUMMARY       NVARCHAR(500),
    ROOT_CAUSE       NVARCHAR(1000),
    RESOLUTION_STEPS NCLOB,
    SEVERITY         NVARCHAR(10),
    AUTO_RECOVERABLE NVARCHAR(5),
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    LAST_SEEN        TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FIRST_SEEN       TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    MPL_ID           NVARCHAR(100),
    RESOLVED         NVARCHAR(5)     DEFAULT 'false'
);

CREATE TABLE ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE (
    FINGERPRINT      NVARCHAR(32)    PRIMARY KEY,
    ERROR_CATEGORY   NVARCHAR(50),
    IFLOW_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
    LAST_ANALYZED    TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    OCCURRENCE_COUNT INTEGER         DEFAULT 1,
    CACHED_ANALYSIS  NCLOB
);

Step 3 — Build the EA_TestHarness iFlow

Create a new Integration Flow in your package. The complete flow structure is shown below.

 

 

The flow has the following step sequence:

  1. ProcessDirect Sender — address: /erroranalyzer/triage
  2. Content Modifier (setParams) — sets iflowName, environment, llmModel as Exchange Properties
  3. Classifier — Groovy script, categorises the error
  4. Sanitizer — Groovy script, masks payload values and generates fingerprint
  5. Router 2 — routes Non-LLM vs LLM-eligible errors
  6. DedupCheck — Groovy script, builds SELECT SQL for HANA lookup
  7. JDBC_SELECT Request Reply — executes SELECT against ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  8. ProcessDedupResult — Groovy script, parses JDBC XML response
  9. Router 3 — routes cache hit vs cache miss
  10. BuildCachedResponse / BuildPrompt — builds response from cache or builds LLM request
  11. Request Reply → AI Adapter — calls Claude via OpenRouter
  12. ParseLLMResponse → BuildEnhancedResponse — structures the AI analysis
  13. BuildEmailBody → Mail Receiver — sends HTML alert email
  14. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  15. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

Content Modifier — setParams configuration

Add a Content Modifier as the first step after Start. In the Exchange Property tab, add:

Property Name Type Value

   
environmentConstantDEV
llmModelConstantopenai/gpt-oss-120b:free  ← externalized, change without redeployment

Step 4 — The Classifier Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Classifier. This is the core of the rules-first approach — pure pattern matching, zero API cost, millisecond response.

Category Trigger Action Hits LLM

CONNECTIVITYConnectException, timeout, connection refusedRETRY_BACKOFFNo
AUTH_FAILUREHTTP 401/403, unauthorized, forbiddenREFRESH_CREDENTIALSNo
PAYLOAD_MALFORMEDSAXParseException, malformed XMLDEAD_LETTERNo
MAPPING_FAILUREMappingException, cannot convert, XSLT errorANALYZEYes
UNKNOWNEverything elseANALYZEYes
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)
    String exClass = (json.exceptionClass ?: "").toString()
    String exMsg   = (json.exceptionMessage ?: "").toString()

    String category; String action; boolean needsLLM = false

    if (exClass.contains("ConnectException") ||
        exMsg =~ /(?i)(timed out|timeout|connection refused|no route to host)/) {
        category = "CONNECTIVITY"; action = "RETRY_BACKOFF"
    } else if (exMsg =~ /(?i)statusCode:\s*40[13]/ ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(unauthorized|forbidden|invalid credentials)/) {
        category = "AUTH_FAILURE"; action = "REFRESH_CREDENTIALS"
    } else if (exClass.contains("SAXParse") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(content is not allowed in prolog|malformed)/) {
        category = "PAYLOAD_MALFORMED"; action = "DEAD_LETTER"
    } else if (exClass.contains("Mapping") ||
               exMsg =~ /(?i)(xslt|mapping|cannot convert)/) {
        category = "MAPPING_FAILURE"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    } else {
        category = "UNKNOWN"; action = "ANALYZE"; needsLLM = true
    }

    message.setProperty("errorCategory",    category)
    message.setProperty("suggestedAction",  action)
    message.setProperty("needsLLM",         needsLLM.toString())
    message.setProperty("exceptionClass",   exClass)
    message.setProperty("exceptionMessage", exMsg)
    message.setProperty("rawPayload",       (json.payload ?: "").toString())
    if (json.mplId)       message.setProperty("mplId",       json.mplId.toString())
    if (json.iflowName)   message.setProperty("iflowName",   json.iflowName.toString())
    if (json.environment) message.setProperty("environment", json.environment.toString())
    return message
}

Step 5 — The Sanitizer Script

Add a Groovy Script step named Sanitizer. This script parses the raw payload — XML or JSON — and replaces every leaf value with a typed token before anything leaves the tenant.

A customer record like this:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-001",
    "amount": null,
    "customer": "John Smith",
    "taxId": "DE123456789"
  }
}

Becomes this — safe to send to any external LLM:

{
  "order": {
    "id": "[STRING:7]",
    "amount": "[EMPTY]",
    "customer": "[STRING:10]",
    "taxId": "[STRING:11]"
  }
}

The Sanitizer also generates a fingerprint: MD5(iFlowName + exceptionClass + errorCategory). Including iFlowName ensures that the same MappingException in two different iFlows produces different fingerprints — preventing wrong cross-iFlow cache hits.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlParser
import groovy.util.XmlNodePrinter
import groovy.util.Node
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import java.security.MessageDigest

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String raw = ((message.getProperty("rawPayload") ?: "").toString()).trim()
    String sanitized
    try {
        if (raw.startsWith("<")) {
            def root = new XmlParser().parseText(raw)
            maskXmlNode(root)
            def sw = new StringWriter()
            new XmlNodePrinter(new PrintWriter(sw)).print(root)
            sanitized = sw.toString().trim()
        } else if (raw.startsWith("{") || raw.startsWith("[")) {
            def obj = new JsonSlurper().parseText(raw)
            sanitized = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(maskJson(obj)))
        } else if (raw.isEmpty()) {
            sanitized = "[EMPTY]"
        } else {
            sanitized = maskValue(raw)
        }
    } catch (Exception e) {
        sanitized = "[UNPARSEABLE: ${e.message}]"
    }
    String fpSource = (message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: "").toString() + "|" +
                      (message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "").toString()
    message.setProperty("sanitizedPayload", sanitized)
    message.setProperty("fingerprint", md5(fpSource))
    return message
}

void maskXmlNode(Node node) {
    if (node.children().any { it instanceof Node }) {
        node.children().each { if (it instanceof Node) maskXmlNode(it) }
    } else { node.setValue(maskValue(node.text())) }
}

def maskJson(obj) {
    if (obj instanceof Map) { def m = [:]; obj.each { k, v -> m[k] = maskJson(v) }; return m }
    else if (obj instanceof List) { return obj.collect { maskJson(it) } }
    else { return maskValue(obj == null ? "" : obj.toString()) }
}

String maskValue(String v) {
    if (v == null || v.trim().isEmpty()) return "[EMPTY]"
    v = v.trim()
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+/)                return "[INT:${v.length()}]"
    if (v ==~ /-?\d+\.\d+/)           return "[DECIMAL]"
    if (v ==~ /\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}.*/) return "[DATE]"
    if (v ==~ /(?i)(true|false)/)     return "[BOOL]"
    return "[STRING:${v.length()}]"
}

String md5(String s) {
    MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5").digest(s.bytes).encodeHex().toString()
}

Step 6 — Router Configuration

Add a Router step after the Sanitizer. Configure two routes:

Router 2 — routes based on whether LLM is needed:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (LLM path)${property.needsLLM} = 'true'Non-XML
Non_LLM (default)Default Route — checkedN/A

Router 3 — routes based on HANA cache result:

Route Name Condition Expression Expression Type

Route 1 (cache hit)${property.cacheHit} = 'true'Non-XML
Default (cache miss)Default Route — checkedN/A

 

 

 

Step 7 — HANA Dedup Check via JDBC

On the LLM path, before calling Claude, the system checks HANA Cloud. Add these three steps in sequence:

  1. DedupCheck — Groovy script that builds the SELECT SQL
  2. Request Reply → JDBC Receiver — executes the query against HANA
  3. ProcessDedupResult — parses the XML response from JDBC

Important: CPI's JDBC adapter returns query results as XML — not JSON. The structure is ROOT → select_response → row. Use groovy.util.XmlSlurper (not groovy.xml.XmlSlurper — that class does not exist in CPI's Groovy runtime).

// ProcessDedupResult — parse JDBC XML response
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String) ?: ""
    try {
        def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)
        def rows = xml.select_response.row
        if (rows && rows.size() > 0) {
            def row = rows[0]
            String cachedAnalysis = row.CACHED_ANALYSIS.text()
            String occCount = row.OCCURRENCE_COUNT.text() ?: "1"
            if (cachedAnalysis) {
                message.setProperty("cacheHit", "true")
                message.setProperty("cachedAnalysis", cachedAnalysis)
                message.setProperty("cacheOccurrenceCount",
                    (occCount.toInteger() + 1).toString())
            } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
        } else { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    } catch(Exception e) { message.setProperty("cacheHit", "false") }
    return message
}

JDBC Receiver Adapter configuration for both SELECT and WRITE steps:

Field Value

JDBC Data Source AliasHANA_Cloud_JDBC
Connection Timeout30 seconds
Query/Response Timeout30 seconds

 

Step 8 — The Undocumented AI Adapter Configuration

This section is the most important technical finding in this post.

If you have tried connecting SAP's AI Adapter (Custom-OpenAI-REST) to OpenRouter or any external LLM endpoint, you have almost certainly encountered this:

401 Unauthorized — Missing Authentication Header

It does not matter how many times you redeploy the Secure Parameter. Basic mode simply does not work with OpenRouter. After extensive testing, I identified the exact combination of three settings that resolves this completely.

Fix 1 — Configuration Type: Advanced (not Basic)

In the Processing tab, change Configuration Type from Basic to Advanced. Basic mode uses SAP's internal OpenAI abstraction layer which is incompatible with OpenRouter's authentication format. Advanced mode gives direct REST control.

 

 

 

Fix 2 — Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter

Store the full Authorization value — not just the key — in the Secure Parameter: Bearer sk-or-v1-xxx. The adapter uses this as the complete Authorization header value. Without the prefix, the header is malformed and the request fails.

Fix 3 — Split the URL across tabs

Connection tab and Processing tab must share the URL:

  • Connection tab Address: https://openrouter.ai
  • Processing tab Relative URL: /api/v1/chat/completions

 

Complete Working Configuration

Connection Tab:

Field Value

Message ProtocolCustom-OpenAI-REST
Addresshttps://openrouter.ai
API Key AliasOpenRouter_API_Key
Connection Timeout60000 ms
Response Timeout60000 ms

 

Processing Tab:

Field Value

Configuration TypeAdvanced ← Critical — Basic fails with 401
HTTP MethodPOST
Relative URL/api/v1/chat/completions
Request Payload SourceExchange Body
Request HeadersHTTP-Referer|X-Title|Content-Type

 

Step 9 — Build the LLM Request (BuildPrompt)

The BuildPrompt Groovy script assembles the request body for Claude. It reads the sanitized context from message properties — never from the raw payload — and forces a structured JSON response format.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    String errorCategory = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: "UNKNOWN"
    String exClass       = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String exMsg         = message.getProperty("exceptionMessage") ?: ""
    String sanitized     = message.getProperty("sanitizedPayload") ?: "[EMPTY]"
    String iflowName     = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown"
    String environment   = message.getProperty("environment") ?: "Unknown"
    String llmModel      = message.getProperty("llmModel") ?: "anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku"

    String userContent = """\
Analyze this SAP CPI runtime error and respond with ONLY valid JSON.

Required structure:
{
  "error_summary": "one clear sentence",
  "root_cause": "specific technical cause",
  "resolution_steps": ["step 1", "step 2", "step 3"],
  "auto_recoverable": false,
  "severity": "HIGH"
}

Severity: HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW only.
auto_recoverable: boolean only.

Error details:
iFlow: ${iflowName}
Environment: ${environment}
Category: ${errorCategory}
Exception class: ${exClass}
Exception message: ${exMsg}
Sanitized payload structure (real values masked):
${sanitized}"""

    def requestBody = [
        model: llmModel,
        messages: [
            [role: "system", content: "You are an SAP CPI integration expert. Respond only with valid JSON."],
            [role: "user",   content: userContent]
        ],
        temperature: 0.2,
        max_tokens: 400
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(requestBody))
    return message
}

Step 10 — EA_VictimFlow: Wiring the Exception Subprocess

EA_VictimFlow demonstrates how to connect any existing iFlow to the ErrorAnalyzer. The pattern is reusable — copy the exception subprocess to any failing iFlow by changing only the iflowName constant.

 

Add a Content Modifier at the start of EA_VictimFlow with these Exchange Properties:

Property Name Type Value

iflowNameConstantEA_VictimFlow
environmentConstantDEV

Inside the Exception Subprocess, add a Groovy script named ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def exCaught   = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")
    String exClass = exCaught ? exCaught.getClass().getName() : "Unknown"
    String exMsg   = exCaught ? exCaught.getMessage() : "Unknown error"
    String mplId   = message.getHeader("SAP_MessageProcessingLogID", String.class) ?: "unavailable"
    String iflowName = message.getProperty("iflowName") ?: "Unknown_iFlow"
    String payload = ""
    try { payload = message.getBody(String) ?: "" } catch(Exception e) { payload = "" }

    def envelope = [
        exceptionClass:   exClass,
        exceptionMessage: exMsg,
        payload:          payload,
        mplId:            mplId,
        iflowName:        iflowName,
        environment:      message.getProperty("environment") ?: "DEV"
    ]
    message.setBody(JsonOutput.toJson(envelope))
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
    return message
}

Connect the ExceptionEnvelopeBuilder to a ProcessDirect Receiver with address: /erroranalyzer/triage

Important: SAP_ApplicationID is NOT the iFlow name — it is the application binding ID. Always use a Content Modifier constant for iflowName as shown above.

Step 10b — Writing to HANA Cloud

After Claude returns the analysis, two separate JDBC write steps persist the intelligence to HANA. They are intentionally split into two steps because CPI's JDBC adapter executes exactly one SQL statement per call — chaining two statements with a semicolon throws a syntax error.

PrepareHANAWrite — writes full analysis to ERROR_PATTERNS

This script builds a MERGE statement that either inserts a new error record or increments the occurrence count if the fingerprint already exists. Note the HANA-specific syntax: WITH PRIMARY KEY is required — standard WHERE syntax fails with a syntax error.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llm = new JsonSlurper().parseText(
                  message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}")

    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")    ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory")  ?: ""
    String exClass     = message.getProperty("exceptionClass") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")      ?: ""
    String environment = message.getProperty("environment")    ?: ""
    String mplId       = message.getProperty("mplId")          ?: ""
    String aiSummary   = (llm.error_summary   ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String rootCause   = (llm.root_cause       ?: "").toString().replace("'","''")
    String steps       = JsonOutput.toJson(llm.resolution_steps ?: []).replace("'","''")
    String severity    = (llm.severity         ?: "MEDIUM").toString()
    String autoRec     = (llm.auto_recoverable ?: false).toString()
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_PATTERNS
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, EXCEPTION_CLASS, IFLOW_NAME,
         ENVIRONMENT, AI_SUMMARY, ROOT_CAUSE, RESOLUTION_STEPS,
         SEVERITY, AUTO_RECOVERABLE, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
         LAST_SEEN, FIRST_SEEN, MPL_ID, RESOLVED)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${exClass}',
                '${iflowName}','${environment}','${aiSummary}',
                '${rootCause}','${steps}','${severity}',
                '${autoRec}', 1,'${now}','${now}','${mplId}','false')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

PrepareDedup — writes fast lookup entry to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def llmRaw         = message.getProperty("llmAnalysis") ?: "{}"
    String fingerprint = message.getProperty("fingerprint")   ?: ""
    String category    = message.getProperty("errorCategory") ?: ""
    String iflowName   = message.getProperty("iflowName")     ?: ""
    String cached      = llmRaw.replace("'","''")
    String now         = new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")

    String sql = """UPSERT ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
        (FINGERPRINT, ERROR_CATEGORY, IFLOW_NAME,
         LAST_ANALYZED, OCCURRENCE_COUNT, CACHED_ANALYSIS)
        VALUES ('${fingerprint}','${category}','${iflowName}',
                '${now}', 1,'${cached}')
        WITH PRIMARY KEY"""

    message.setBody(sql)
    return message
}

The complete write sequence after Claude analysis:

  1. BuildEnhancedResponse — assembles the final JSON response
  2. BuildEmailBody — builds the HTML alert email
  3. Mail Receiver — dispatches the email notification
  4. PrepareHANAWrite → JDBC_WRITE — persists to ERROR_PATTERNS
  5. PrepareDedup → JDBC_WRITE2 — persists to ERROR_DEDUP_CACHE
  6. End

Both JDBC_WRITE and JDBC_WRITE2 use the same receiver adapter configuration as JDBC_SELECT  Data Source Alias: HANA_Cloud_JDBC, timeouts: 30 seconds.

Email is dispatched before the HANA writes so that if HANA is unavailable (free tier stops nightly), the developer notification still goes out regardless.

Step 11 — Testing

Deploy both iFlows. Test using any REST client by POSTing to the EA_VictimFlow HTTPS endpoint with plain text body:

Request Body Error Category Expected Behaviour

mappingMAPPING_FAILUREHits Claude, writes to HANA, sends email with AI analysis
authAUTH_FAILURENo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
timeoutCONNECTIVITYNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email
xmlPAYLOAD_MALFORMEDNo LLM, sends lightweight known-error alert email

Results

First Run — MAPPING_FAILURE (new error, hits Claude)

{
  "fingerprint":     "dfb60537dd72852be164ee7f46bab45a",
  "mplId":           "AGoi2tHxrNFx-9CloVUzH46UcB-6",
  "iflowName":       "EA_VictimFlow",
  "environment":     "DEV",
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "suggestedAction": "ANALYZE",
  "sanitizedPayload": "{\"order\":{\"id\":\"[STRING:7]\",\"amount\":\"[EMPTY]\"}}",
  "servedFromCache": "false",
  "llmParseSuccess": "true",
  "aiAnalysis": {
    "summary":   "Mapping failed because null Amount field cannot be converted to decimal",
    "rootCause": "Source system sent null where decimal value was expected",
    "resolutionSteps": [
      "Validate Amount field in source payload before mapping",
      "Add null check in Groovy mapping script before decimal conversion",
      "Define default value for optional numeric fields"
    ],
    "autoRecoverable": false,
    "severity": "HIGH"
  }
}

 

Second Run — Same Error, HANA Cache Hit

{
  "errorCategory":   "MAPPING_FAILURE",
  "servedFromCache": "true",
  "occurrenceCount": "2",
  "aiAnalysis": { ...same analysis returned instantly, zero LLM call... }
}

 

The real MPL ID is captured in every response — developers can jump directly to the processing log without searching.

HANA Cloud as a Learning Store

Every new error diagnosis is permanently stored. After running for several weeks:

-- Top recurring failures
SELECT IFLOW_NAME, ERROR_CATEGORY, OCCURRENCE_COUNT,
       AI_SUMMARY, SEVERITY, FIRST_SEEN, LAST_SEEN
FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
ORDER BY OCCURRENCE_COUNT DESC;

-- All unresolved HIGH severity errors
SELECT * FROM ERROR_PATTERNS
WHERE SEVERITY = 'HIGH' AND RESOLVED = 'false'
ORDER BY LAST_SEEN DESC;

 

This gives you a persistent error intelligence dashboard that no standard CPI monitoring tool provides natively. The more errors the system sees, the richer this store becomes.

Email Notifications

Every error  known or novel  triggers an email alert to the development team. Known errors (auth, timeout, malformed) receive a lightweight notification with category and suggested action. Novel errors with Claude analysis receive the full HTML email with root cause, resolution steps, and MPL ID link.

 

 

Honest Limitations

This is a V1 implementation on a trial tenant. To be transparent:

  • Synchronous design: The LLM call adds 3–8 seconds to the exception path. Production deployment should decouple via JMS queue — not available on trial tenants.
  • Exception message not sanitized: The payload is fully masked but exception message text is sent as-is. Real SAP exceptions can embed field values. Exception message sanitization is planned for V2.
  • No auto-remediation: suggestedAction is informational only. Phase B adds deterministic auto-recovery actions (retry, credential refresh) for known error types.
  • Free model availability: OpenRouter free models go offline without notice. For production, use SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub or a paid OpenRouter tier.

What Is Next: Joule Studio Integration via MCP

Phase 2 exposes the ErrorAnalyzer as an MCP tool and connects it to SAP Joule Studio via the MCP Gateway announced at SAP Sapphire 2026. The goal: ask Joule in natural language — "What failed in my OrderProcessing flow in the last hour?" — and have it call the ErrorAnalyzer tool, query the HANA learning store, and return a conversational diagnosis grounded in your tenant's actual error history.

Phase 3 replaces the exact-match fingerprint with semantic vector similarity using HANA Cloud's NLP engine , already enabled on the instance. Similar errors that differ only in variable values will match correctly, dramatically improving cache hit rates and diagnosis quality.

Summary

The ErrorAnalyzer Agent demonstrates that AI error handling in SAP CPI does not have to be expensive, risky, or shallow.

  • A rules-first classifier keeps 75% of errors away from the LLM entirely — zero cost, millisecond response
  • A payload sanitizer ensures no business data leaves the tenant — the LLM sees shapes, not values
  • A HANA dedup cache means the same error is diagnosed once — every subsequent occurrence is instant and free
  • A HANA learning store accumulates error intelligence permanently — the system gets smarter and cheaper the longer it runs
  • The AI Adapter breakthrough — Advanced mode, Bearer prefix in Secure Parameter, split URL — is the practical finding I hope saves other developers the hours of debugging it cost to discover

If you build on this or encounter any issues replicating the AI Adapter configuration, please comment below. The complete Groovy scripts are available on request.

entry.title: Re: Fiori Generator error: "Edmx metadata is required" us...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support and here's the response. Please check the suggestions:

When generating a SAP Fiori application using the @SAP/fiori:app generator in Visual Studio Code, encountering an error related to Edmx metadata can be frustrating. Here are some steps and considerations to help resolve this issue:

Possible Causes and Solutions

  1. Missing Business Role or Catalog Assignment:

    • Ensure that your business user has the necessary roles and catalog assignments in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Missing roles can prevent access to required services.
    • Check if the business catalog related to the service you are trying to access is assigned to your user. For example, if you are dealing with service orders, ensure the catalog like SAP_CRM_BC_SERV_PROCORDER_PC is assigned. For more information, see 150696 - Error "App could not be opened either due to an incorrect SAP Fiori launchpad configuration or a missing role assignment".

  2. Communication Scenario Configuration:

    • Verify that the communication scenario is correctly set up to allow access to the OData services. This includes ensuring that the communication arrangement is properly configured.

  3. Reentrance Ticket Authentication:

    • Double-check the reentrance ticket setup. Ensure that the system URL and credentials are correctly entered and that the authentication method is supported. For more information, see SAP Community: SAP Fiori tools 2406 update includes added support for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and more.

  4. Service Activation:

    • Make sure the OData service you are trying to access is active and published. Inactive services can lead to metadata fetching issues.

  5. Network and Browser Settings:

    • Ensure that there are no browser pop-ups or network settings blocking the authentication process.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: System based assessment issue on Submitting SAP Certification C_CPE_2601 SAP CAP Backend Develop

I feel the conceptualizing knowledge is not evaluated in these formats and no transparency in the exam format, basically no scope of growth for a candidate if failed in an exam.

entry.title: Simple RAP managed app with Abstract entity popup.
entry.content:
This post is to help those who are interested in learning SAP RAP application development. I have tried to cover the concepts of Validation, Determination, Actions, Draft handling, Authorization control, Late numbering and Side effects in this application.

This is a application to manage student data covering the below concepts.

Validation: It verifies the data before saving the same to the database. In our example, we will be checking if the marks entered is positive.

Determination: It automatically calculates or derives field values. It gets executed before Validation in RAP lifecycle. In our example, we will be filling the "created by" and "percentage" fields using determination logic. 

Actions: It is used to achieve custom business operations that are beyond standard CRUD(Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations. In our example, we will be showing a popup for data update using action.

Draft handling: It is used to save work-in-progress without committing changes to the persistent database table. In our example, draft handling would be enabled.

Authorization control: It is used to control who is allowed to access the BO application. In our example, we will see how Global authorization is handled.

Numbering: It is used to generate key of business object. In our example, we will see "Later numbering" concept implemented.

Etag: It is used for Optimistic locking to provide concurrency control. 

Popup: Creating POPUP using Abstract entity.

Lets get started with the application.

Step1:  Create database table in eclipse.

@EndUserText.label : 'Student table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zjd_student_tbl {
  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key id     : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  name       : abap.char(30);
  address    : abap.char(50);
  marks      : abap.int1;
  percentage : abap.int1;
  createdby  : abap.char(15);
  createdon  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

 

Step2: Create Interface root CDS view entity.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for Student table'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZI_STUDENT_TBL
  as select from zjd_student_tbl
{
  key id         as Id,
      name       as Name,
      address    as Address,
      marks      as Marks,
      percentage as Percentage,
      createdby  as Createdby,
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      createdon  as Createdon
}

 

Step3: Create projection root CDS view entity on top of Interface root view.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view on ZI_STUDENT_TBL'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define root view entity ZC_STUDENT_TBL
  provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_STUDENT_TBL
{
    key Id,
    Name,
    Address,
    Marks,
    Percentage,
    Createdby,
    @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
    Createdon
}

 

Step 4: Create Abstract entity. The same will be used for displaying popup to the user for editing the records.

@EndUserText.label: 'Student POPUP abstract entity'
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define abstract entity ZA_STUD_EDIT
{
  name       : abap.char(30);
  address    : abap.char(50);
  marks      : abap.int1;
}

 

Step 5: Create behavior definition for Interface view.

managed implementation in class zbp_i_student_tbl unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft; //For Draft

define behavior for ZI_STUDENT_TBL 
persistent table zjd_student_tbl
draft table zjd_student_d //For Draft
with additional save// For additional save
lock master
total etag Createdon //For Draft
authorization master ( global, instance )
etag master Createdon
late numbering
{
  create ( authorization : global );
  update;
  delete;

  draft action Edit; //For Draft
  draft action Activate optimized; //For Draft
  draft action Discard; //For Draft
  draft action Resume; //For Draft
  draft determine action Prepare; //For Draft

  action updatestud parameter ZA_STUD_EDIT result [1] $self;

  determination setcreatedby on save { field id; create; }

  determination setPercentage on modify { field marks; update; }

  validation validatepercentage on save { field id; update; create; }

  side effects { field Marks affects field Percentage; }

  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( readonly ) Createdby;
  field ( readonly ) Createdon;
  field ( readonly ) Percentage;

  mapping for zjd_student_tbl
    {
      Id         = id;
      Name       = name;
      Address    = address;
      Marks      = marks;
      Percentage = percentage;
      Createdby  = createdby;
      Createdon  = createdon;
    }
}

 

Step 6: Create Behavior implementation class(Quick fix CTRL + 1) will create the class. Go to 'Local Types' tab to see the Handler and Saver class.

i. Late numbering code will be done in Saver class method "adjust numbers".

CLASS lsc_zi_student_tbl DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
    METHODS adjust_numbers REDEFINITION.
    METHODS save_modified REDEFINITION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_zi_student_tbl IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD adjust_numbers.
    SELECT MAX( id ) FROM zjd_student_tbl INTO @DATA(lv_id).
    IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
      lv_id += 1.
    ELSE.
      lv_id = 1.
    ENDIF.
    LOOP AT mapped-zi_student_tbl REFERENCE INTO DATA(ls_num).
      ls_num->Id = lv_id.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD save_modified.
    DATA: lt_student_tbl TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zjd_student_tbl,
          lt_student_log TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zjd_student_log.
    IF create-zi_student_tbl IS NOT INITIAL.
      lt_student_log = CORRESPONDING #( create-zi_student_tbl ).
      LOOP AT lt_student_log ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_student_log>).
        <ls_student_log>-changing_operation = 'CREATE'.
        GET TIME STAMP FIELD <ls_student_log>-created_at.
      ENDLOOP.
      INSERT zjd_student_log FROM TABLE @LT_student_log.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

ii.  The following methods in Handler class will help us in Action, Determination, Validation, Authorization handling.

CLASS lhc_ZI_STUDENT_TBL DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_student_tbl RESULT result.
    METHODS get_global_authorizations FOR GLOBAL AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_student_tbl RESULT result.
    METHODS updatestud FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_student_tbl~updatestud RESULT result.
    METHODS setcreatedby FOR DETERMINE ON SAVE
      IMPORTING keys FOR zi_student_tbl~setcreatedby.
    METHODS validatepercentage FOR VALIDATE ON SAVE
      IMPORTING keys FOR zi_student_tbl~validatepercentage.
    METHODS setpercentage FOR DETERMINE ON MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR zi_student_tbl~setpercentage.
ENDCLASS.

Authorization: For demo, I have just restricted based on username.

METHOD get_global_authorizations.
    IF requested_authorizations-%create EQ if_abap_behv=>mk-on AND sy-uname EQ <Your_user_name>.
      result-%create = if_abap_behv=>auth-unauthorized.
    ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

Update student data based on popup.

METHOD updatestud.
    DATA(lv_new_name) = keys[ 1 ]-%param-name.
    DATA(lv_new_address) = keys[ 1 ]-%param-address.
    DATA(lv_new_percentage) = keys[ 1 ]-%param-marks.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zi_student_tbl
    UPDATE FIELDS ( Name Address Percentage )
    WITH VALUE #( ( %tky = keys[ 1 ]-%tky
                    Name = lv_new_name
                    Address = lv_new_address
                    Marks = lv_new_percentage
                    Percentage = lv_new_percentage  ) ).

    READ ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zi_student_tbl
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_res).
    result = VALUE #( FOR <lfs_data> IN lt_res (
                      %tky = <lfs_data>-%tky
                      %param = <lfs_data> ) ).
ENDMETHOD.

 Determination to set 'Created by' and 'Percentage': 

METHOD setcreatedby.
    READ ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zi_student_tbl
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_result).

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zi_student_tbl
    UPDATE FIELDS ( Createdby )
    WITH VALUE #( (  %tky = keys[ 1 ]-%tky
                     Createdby = sy-uname
                     ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.
METHOD setPercentage.
    READ ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zi_student_tbl
      ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_result).

    LOOP AT lt_result ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_result>).
      IF <ls_result>-Percentage NE <ls_result>-marks .
        MODIFY ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
         ENTITY zi_student_tbl
         UPDATE FIELDS ( Marks Percentage  )
         WITH VALUE #( (  %tky = keys[ 1 ]-%tky
                          marks = lt_result[ 1 ]-Marks
                          Percentage = lt_result[ 1 ]-Marks ) ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

Validatation:

METHOD validatepercentage.
    READ ENTITIES OF zi_student_tbl IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zi_student_tbl
    ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_result).
    IF lt_result[ 1 ]-Marks < 20.
      APPEND VALUE #(  %tky = lt_result[ 1 ]-%tky ) TO failed-zi_student_tbl.

      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = lt_result[ 1 ]-%tky
                      %msg = new_message_with_text(
                        severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
                        text = 'validation failed'
                      )
                    ) TO reported-zi_student_tbl.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

Step 7: Create Metadata extension file for the projection view(By right clicking the projection view "ZC_STUDENT_TBL)".

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate entity ZC_STUDENT_TBL with
{
  @ui.facet: [{ id: 'StudentPage',
                purpose: #STANDARD,
                label: 'Student Overview',
                type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }]
  @ui.lineItem: [{
      type: #FOR_ACTION,
      dataAction: 'updatestud',
      label: 'Update Student',
      position: 10

   }] //this piece of code is for displaying popup
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'ID' }]
  Id;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:10, label: 'Name' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Name' }]
  Name;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:20, label: 'Address' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Address' }]
  Address;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:30, label: 'Marks' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Marks' }]
  Marks;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:35, label: 'Percentage' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 45, label: 'Percentage' }]
  Percentage;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:40, label: 'Created By' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Created By' }]
  Createdby;
  @ui.lineItem: [{ position:50, label: 'Created On' }]
  @ui.identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Created On' }]
  Createdon;
}

 

Step 8: Create Metadata extension file for Abstract view.

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate entity ZA_STUD_EDIT
    with 
{
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 10  }]
    name;
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    address;
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    marks;    
}

 

Step 9: Behavior definition for consumption view.

projection;
strict ( 2 );
use draft; //For Draft
use side effects;
define behavior for ZC_STUDENT_TBL //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action updatestud;

  use action Activate; //For Draft
  use action Discard;  //For Draft
  use action Edit;     //For Draft
  use action Prepare;  //For Draft
  use action Resume;   //For Draft
}

 

Step 10: Create Service Definition.

@EndUserText.label: 'Student service definition'
define service ZSD_STUDENT_DEMO {
  expose ZC_STUDENT_TBL;
}

 

Step 11: Create Service Binding.

After all the above steps are performed. The below components should be ready.

 

Step12: Publish and preview the application.

Output: Line item display.

Popup to update data

In this blog, we explored the development of a SAP RAP application by implementing several important RAP concepts within a Student Data Management scenario. We covered how validations ensure data integrity, determinations automate field derivation, actions enable custom business operations, and draft handling manages work-in-progress data. We also demonstrated global authorization control, late numbering for key generation, ETag-based optimistic locking for concurrency control, and popup implementation using abstract entities.

By combining these features, SAP RAP enables developers to build modern, cloud-ready, and enterprise-grade applications with less custom code while following SAP's clean-core principles. Understanding these concepts is essential for designing robust and scalable RAP applications in SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP environments.

I hope this blog provides a practical starting point for your RAP learning journey.

Keep exploring! Keep learning 🙂

entry.title: Re: Joule Booster – "Setting Up Subaccount(s)" Step FAILED | SAP BTP - public cloud

entry.title: REHIRE WITH OLD EMPLOYMENT REPLICATION TO ECP
entry.content:
Currently, rehiring old employees processed via 'Rehire with Old Employment' replicate to EC Payroll prematurely before the 'Manage Pending Hires' workflow is completed. Since this happens before the hire is officially approved and saved in Employee Central, it poses a high risk of payroll errors.

Expected as per KBA - 3736423 - How to identify and stop the data replication from happening for rehires(ROOE) whose MPH is not completed - Onboarding

I see that there is also influence on this - https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/346208/?section=sectionDetails

or those who have encountered this scenario with EC Payroll integration, may I ask:

  • What workarounds or processes are you currently using to prevent premature replication for rehiring old employees?

  • Are there any manual steps or configuration adjustments you perform in 'Manage Pending Hires' to mitigate this issue until the hire is officially saved in Employee Central?

Any insights  practices you can share would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Analytical Query Inquiry
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

The issue you are experiencing with the Goods Issue quantity showing as 14 instead of 10 can be attributed to the way data is aggregated in your analytical query. This discrepancy often occurs due to the use of attributes from associations that have a ‘non-to-1’ cardinality. For more information, see the following knowledge base article: 3166853 - A custom analytical query results in larger values than expected.

Explanation:

  • Cardinality Issue: When two tables are joined with a cardinality of X 1->n Y, entries from table X can appear multiple times, leading to duplicated data in the results. This is likely why the Goods Issue quantity is incorrectly aggregating values from Goods Movement Type 101.

  • Resolution: To resolve this, you should review the associations in your query and ensure that they are correctly set to avoid duplication. Removing fields that contribute to data duplication can help achieve accurate results.

Steps to Fix:

  1. Review Associations: Check the associations between your tables and ensure they are set correctly. Avoid using fields from ‘to many’ associations unless necessary.
  2. Adjust Query: Remove or adjust fields that are causing duplication in your query.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Conversational Analytics in SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Conversational Analytics in SAP Joule: Unlocking Business Insights Through Natural Language Integration with SAP Analytics Cloud

By Ramkumar Arumugasamy

In today’s digital landscape, intuitive insights and intelligent assistance are becoming essential for business users. SAP Joule, the AI-powered digital assistant from SAP, takes this a step further by enabling natural-language queries and actionable insights across enterprise applications. One of the most impactful capabilities of Joule is its ability to connect with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) - bringing conversational analytics directly into business workflows.

This blog provides a simplified overview of how SAP Joule is integrated with SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

Prerequisites for Joule Onboarding

  • Entitlements for Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning.
  • To support navigation to different applications from Joule and handle role-based access scenarios, you need to configure the navigation service of SAP Build Work Zone.

Note: By default, SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition uses the SAP Authentication and Trust Management service (XSUAA) for identity authentication. Therefore, we recommend first switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication as the authentication mechanism.

Required Applications/Services

  • Joule à Foundation
  • SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition à Foundation or Standard

Here are the steps for configuring Joule:

  1. Configure Destination to Use Navigation Service by creating a Navigation service destination
  2. Configure Trust to the Identity Authentication Tenant by adding the IAS URL in the joule subaccount trusted configuration
  3. Configure User Attributes for Joule from the Identity Directory
  4. Configure Trusted Domains in Identity Authentication
  5. Integration Process:

In the current release of Analytical Pattern in Joule, at least one existing Joule setup is required for any line of business (LoB), such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, or SAP Build Work Zone. Joule can also be used in stand-alone mode.

Here, we are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System.

Since most SAP customers already have Joule activated, we address both configuration options below.

Option 1

  • Create a new SAP BTP Subaccount.
  • Run Joule booster

 

After completing the Joule Booster process, you will see the Joule formation in Ready status.

 

This completes the Joule activation for analytical insights. However, additional configuration steps are required.

Option 2

  • Within your SAP BTP Global Account, navigate to Formations, and search for the Joule formation you have already set up. You can click on Include System.

 

 

  • Review the included system. The system is now ready, which completes the Joule activation for analytical insights.

6. Post-integration checks

  • Once we follow the steps above, you can validate if the BTP Destination is created in your SAP BTP Subaccount. Destination named – “DYN_SAC_SSO”. Also, verify that the OAuth Client was created in your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

 

  • In your SAP Analytics Cloud, you should see a new line item for Joule in the SAC Roles. Ensure that the Read option is selected after the Joule Integration.

7. Create an OAuth Client for the Design Time in SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create OAuth client under the Configured Clients. 

 

 

  • Using this certificate, create a Trusted Identity Provider named Joule_IDP_Manual in SAC

8. Consume Role Definition Content from SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Note: Store the Secret value for DT and RT securely, as it will not be visible later.

9. Create a Design Time Destination in your SAP BTP Subaccount

After creating the destination, in additional Properties add the below properties.

HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false &

URL.headers.x-sap-sac-custom-auth: true

10. Create a Runtime Destination by Cloning in our SAP BTP Subaccount

Select the existing DYN_SAC_SSO destination from BTP Joule subaccount and choose Duplicate to create a new destination based of it named (SAC_SWZ_RT)

11. Create a Content Provider in SAP Build Work Zone

To create a content provider in the SAP Build Work Zone, ensure you have the "Launchpad_Admin" role assigned.

 

12. Provision Users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone Using IPS

Note: This process provisions users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone. You will be working in SAP Cloud Identity Services and will need Administrator access.

 

  • Setup SAP SAC as a Source System in IAS
  • Setup SAP Build Work Zone as a Target System in IAS

 

 

13. Run User and Group Provisioning Job in IAS

              Check provisioning Logs to see the status.

14. Validate Provisioning

  • Open Build work zone application from BTP
  • Go to Channel Manager and refresh it to validate the provisioning.

15. Index Your Data Using Just Ask and Test Your Integration

The data available for your business users to ask Joule analytical questions is the data from supported models indexed by the just ask feature in your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant. Analytical insights support the following indexed models:

  • Models acquired from external sources and imported into SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Analytic models from SAP Datasphere that are part of an SAP Business Data Cloud formation that also includes your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

Your users will need different permissions in SAP Analytics Cloud depending on their role:

  • To index models in the just ask feature of SAP Analytics Cloud, users will need the Maintain permission for the Just Ask privilege.
  • To ask questions to Joule's analytical insights feature and view answers and charts, users need the Read permission for the Joule privilege.

16. Open your S/4HANA Public Cloud instance and launch Joule

Query: - Give me a chart of discount per product Coca-Cola and per store

Conclusion: -

The integration of SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) represents a significant advancement in making business intelligence more accessible through natural language interactions. By leveraging SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Cloud Identity Services, and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, organizations can enable conversational analytics that empowers users to explore data, generate insights, and visualize results without requiring deep technical expertise.

Successful implementation requires careful configuration of authentication, trust relationships, destinations, OAuth clients, content providers, and user provisioning. Once integrated, Joule can seamlessly access indexed SAC models through the Just Ask capability, allowing users to retrieve analytical insights, charts, and business information through simple conversational queries.

This integration enhances user productivity, accelerates decision-making, and promotes self-service analytics by bringing AI-driven insights directly into daily business workflows. As organizations continue their digital transformation journey, SAP Joule and SAP Analytics Cloud together provide a powerful foundation for intelligent, data-driven enterprise operations.

entry.title: Etag(Entity Tag) in RAP
entry.content:
Etag is used to achieve Optimistic concurrency control.

Now comes the question : What is Optimistic Concurrency?

Optimistic concurrency control ensures data consistency by preventing lost updates when multiple users read the same data simultaneously and attempt to modify it.

Scenario without ETag:

User  A  and user B reading the same record from database

User A update the data and save the record in database.

User B without knowing data has been already updated, updates the same record and saves the data , at this time data saved by user A will be overwritten by data of user B.  This will result in data inconsistency. To handle such scenario we will go for ETag (Entity tag) .

Scenario with ETag:

For implementing ETag,  we will include a ETag field which will uniquely  identify each updating of the record whenever its updated.

User  A  and user B reading the same record from database. Both get ETag value E1.

User A update the data and save the record in database. Sets the ETag value to E2.

User B without knowing data has been already updated, tries to update the same record but this time the Etag value in database is E2 which is not matching with ETag value in E1. So, update fails for User B with error code 412. This will ensure data consistency.

Steps to implement ETag in RAP:

Step1: Take one field of the database table with datatype ‘abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl’. This would be our etag field.

@EndUserText.label : 'Student table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zjd_student_tbl {
  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key id     : sysuuid_x16 not null;
  name       : abap.char(30);
  address    : abap.char(50);
  marks      : abap.int1;
  percentage : abap.int1;
  createdby  : abap.char(15);
  createdon  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Step2: Add “etag master Createdon” in behaviour definition

Step3: Make the field read only.

Once the above three steps are done, RAP ensures optimistic concurrency control.

By leveraging ETags, SAP RAP provides an efficient and scalable approach to concurrency management. Understanding and implementing ETags is essential for building robust, reliable, and enterprise-ready RAP applications that can safely handle concurrent user updates.

Keep learning! Keep exploring 🙂

entry.title: Re: Digital Signature – Signature Validation Error Using...
entry.content:
I think, you can switch to SSF_KRN_SIGN considering the below features

Feature

SSF_KRN_SIGNSSFW_KRN_SIGN
Output FormatPrimarily PKCS#7 (creates a secured digital envelope).PKCS#1-V1.5 (generates a raw signature).
Signature SizeLarger. Because it includes metadata and certificates, the size varies per run.Smaller and fixed-length (e.g., 128 bytes) depending on your key length.
External IntegrationTricky. The PKCS#7 format wraps raw signatures with timestamps, making verification fail if an external system expects a static, raw hash.Easier. Many third-party systems (like banks or OpenSSL servers) directly support raw PKCS#1-V1.5 hashes.
Setup & ConfigUses SSFA tables and transaction STRUST to configure PSE profiles.Allows you to pass specific algorithms/formats directly in the parameters.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 09 - Injecting conditions via filters
What is the best practice for creating Communication arrangement, communication user
entry.content:
I work in a rollout SAP public cloud project . I am setting up communication arragenemnt, user for rollout company.

When I check the system, I see that there is already commication arrangement active for scenario SAM COM 0466 for headquarter. Should I create a another one for branch company? or should I use the exsiting one? What is the best practice for this? Please advise.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: OData vs SOAP for .NET WMS Integration in SAP Public...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

To integrate your .NET-based WMS solution with SAP Business ByDesign, you can utilize OData services and SOAP APIs for real-time warehouse tasks. Here are some recommended APIs:

  • SiteLogisticsTask: This API is useful for managing logistics tasks such as Goods Receipt and Stock Transfer. It provides real-time capabilities and is suitable for high-frequency requests typical of scanning applications.
  • WarehouseConfirmation: This API can be used for confirming warehouse tasks like picking and packing. It is designed to handle high-volume requests efficiently.

These APIs are generally robust and perform well under high-frequency loads, but it is essential to monitor and optimize your application’s request patterns to ensure smooth operation.

2. Public Cloud WM Capability & Responsiveness

The Warehouse Management module in SAP Public Cloud offers flexibility similar to on-premise WM/EWM, but there are some considerations:

  • API-triggered Movements: The Public Cloud WM module supports API-triggered movements, allowing for integration  with third-party systems like your .NET application. For more information, see SAP S/4HANA warehousing options - SAP Community.
  • Performance Limitations: While SAP Public Cloud is designed to handle high-volume requests, there may be throttling limits to prevent system overload. It is advisable to check SAP’s documentation for specific limits and optimize your application’s request frequency.
  • Complex Scenarios: The Public Cloud WM architecture provides sufficient coverage for complex scenarios when managed via external APIs. However, it is crucial to ensure that your integration respects the ‘Clean Core’ strategy to maintain system reliability.

For technical documentation and API maps, you can refer to SAP’s API Business Hub and other SAP community resources.

APIs for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Stock Inconsistency in MC.9 Report
entry.content:
Why MB52/MMBE show 0 but MC.9 shows 4 is because

  • MB52/MMBE → These reports pull directly from the current stock tables (e.g., MARD, MARC, etc.). They always reflect the real-time physical stock in the system.
  • MC.9 (Inventory Analysis) → This transaction uses information structures (S031, S032, etc.) that are updated via LIS (Logistics Information System). These structures are not always in sync with the real-time stock tables.
  • If there was a posting error, reversal, or update failure, the LIS info structure may still show quantities even though the actual stock is zero.


Perform the below steps to resolve

  • Run OMCN → Reorganize LIS structures for the relevant info structure (S031/S032).
  • Use Transaction OMCQ to check for update errors in LIS.
  • Compare MB5B (Stock on posting date) to see if historical postings explain the 4 units.
  • If confirmed as inconsistency, run RMCSIS00 (Rebuild LIS info structures) to realign MC.9 with actual stock.

entry.title: SAP Add-On Assembly Kit 6.0
entry.content:
Yes, 6.0, no typo 😊 - there is a new version of the SAP Add-On Assembly Kit available since a few days. The Add-On Assembly Kit goes App

Main change in the new version is that you can now use the Add-On Assembly App instead of the good old SDA (Software Delivery Assembler) to create the installation file.

This app is hosted and managed by SAP. It comes with the following features:

  •  A wizard to define the requirements or prerequisites for the installation of your add-on. Only if they are met, you customers can install the add-on
  • A direct connection to SAP‘s product database which makes sure that you can only select valid products and combinations when defining the prerequisites
  • As input for the app, SDC provides a delivery file with the installed software component releases, support package levels, installed languages, and the installed SAP_BASIS release of your assembly system

Documentation of all the details is available on the SAP Help Portal at SAP Add-On Assembly Kit.

Now, we would love to hear from you. Did you try it? Are the new options helpful? Where should we go next?

Please help us moving into the right direction and provide your feedback! Best way to do so: join our Customer Engagement Initiative New version of SAP Add-On Assembly Kit.

Registration is open until 20. June 2026

Thanks a lot and looking forward to your thoughts!

entry.title: Re: OMJI changes are not capturing into a TR.
entry.content:
If the system is not automatically prompting you for a transport request, or if you need to create one manually for your OMJI changes, follow these steps:

  1. Access the Request Tool:
    Navigate to the transport organizer via transaction SE09 or SE10.
  2. Create a Request:
    Click the Create icon (F5), select the Customizing Request radio button, and hit Continue.
  3. Add the Transport Description:
    Provide a short description (e.g., "OMJI Lock Settings Update") and save. Note the generated Request number.
  4. Link in OMJI:
    Instead of just saving within OMJI, go to the top menu bar in the transaction and select Edit > Transport > Include in Request. Paste or select the Customizing Request you just created.

entry.title: Re: 最新的公有云版本是否支持内部订单？
entry.content:
Hi Liuhu,

Short answer: No — internal orders are still not supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, including the latest release. The information you heard is not correct.

This is not a recent change in either direction. The Manage Internal Orders app was already removed as of the 1902 release, and as of release 1905 new customers can no longer create internal orders. The official position is documented in SAP Note 2853554

What to use instead: The successor object is the WBS element within Enterprise Projects. The features and functions of the former internal-order app are covered by the Project Control and Project Builder apps; the relevant scope item is Project Financial Control (1NT). For investment/CIP scenarios that on-premise would have used investment internal orders, the standard Public Cloud approach is Investment Projects managed via WBS elements acting as settlement senders/receivers for CIP and Asset under Construction postings.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Modern ABAP Control Break Processing Using LOOP AT GROUP BY and REDUCE Operator
entry.content:
I'm not used to REDUCE, and I am always frustrated when trying/using grouped LOOPs, so I'm not the best one to give his humble opinion here.

(I'd approach your "problem" with internal SQL instructions, so imagine XD)

Once said that: CLEAN ABAP is the way. Inline declarations for your table (you don't need to TYPE it), field aliases to make the code easy to read and follow and, for Pete's sake, no more hungarian naming.

Your first 32 lines of code could look like

SELECT matnr as material_number,
       matkl as material_group,
       mtart as material_type,
       ntgew as net_weight "where's the unit?????
  FROM mara
  ORDER BY matnr
  INTO TABLE @DATA(material_data)
  UP TO 10 ROWS.

and you can imagine the rest of the program...

entry.title: Re: can special stock B indicator material can be used on...
entry.content: The standard SAP way is to transfer consignment stock (411 B) into your own stock before confirmation. That way, MFBF sees it as unrestricted and can consume it. If business insists on consuming directly from consignment during backflush, you’ll need a custom enhancement, because standard repetitive manufacturing does not support special stock indicators in backflush.

entry.title: How to Get Started with SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance
entry.content:
Why Product Compliance Matters

Companies must navigate an increasingly complex regulatory landscape while ensuring that products can be manufactured, transported, and sold across global markets. Managing compliance efficiently is essential to reducing risk, avoiding disruptions, and maintaining market access.

SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance helps organizations integrate compliance processes directly into their business operations, enabling greater transparency and control across the product lifecycle.

With SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance, businesses can:

  • Manage regulatory compliance requirements centrally
  • Support product marketability across regions and markets
  • Maintain compliance data throughout the value chain
  • Classify and manage dangerous goods effectively
  • Improve collaboration between compliance, product, and logistics teams

What You’ll Learn in This Learning Journey

This beginner-level learning journey consists of three courses that provide a comprehensive introduction to product compliance:

  1. Understanding SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance
    Discover the key capabilities and business benefits of SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance and how it helps organizations meet regulatory requirements.
  2. Compliance Data Foundation and Product Marketability
    Learn how the Compliance Data Foundation supports product compliance processes and explore concepts related to product marketability and regulatory content management.
  3. Dangerous Goods Management
    Understand how to classify dangerous goods, manage regulatory requirements, and configure compliance processes to support safe transportation and handling.

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is ideal for:

  • Architects designing compliance-focused business processes
  • Business Users working with product and regulatory data
  • Consultants supporting compliance implementations
  • Sales and Presales Professionals positioning compliance solutions

No prerequisites are required, making it a great starting point for anyone new to product compliance.

Build a strong foundation in product compliance and regulatory management — explore the Learning Journey “Introducing SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance” here.

entry.title: SAP Transformation Days 2026: A Global Success Story in Cloud ERP Transformation
entry.content:
The SAP Transformation Day series has established itself as a cornerstone event for organizations navigating their journey to SAP Cloud ERP. Co-hosted by SAP SE and regional User Groups since 2025 and continuing throughout 2026, this initiative has successfully brought together over 1,100 customers across multiple continents, creating a powerful platform for knowledge sharing, strategic guidance, and community collaboration.

Each SAP Transformation Day provides a comprehensive overview of SAP's strategy and roadmap for a successful transition to SAP Cloud ERP. Participants gain a clear understanding of how SAP's Cloud ERP portfolio – including both Private Cloud and Public Cloud deployment options – fits into the broader SAP’s strategy and what this means for their organization's digital transformation journey.

The SAP Transformation Day 2026 has already made its mark across diverse regions, demonstrating SAP's commitment to supporting customers worldwide in their digital transformation journey:
Asia Pacific, Germany,  Latin America, Nordics, South Africa, United Kingdom, and a special Pre-Conference session at SAPPHIRE with ASUG.

With upcoming events scheduled in the Netherlands , Mexico, Columbia the momentum continues to build, bringing SAP's Cloud ERP strategy directly to customers in their local markets.

What Makes SAP Transformation Day Unique?

Strategic Partnership with User Groups

The co-hosting model with regional User Groups ensures that these events are not just top-down in The event also featured testimonials from User Group members who have already completed their transformations, offering attendees real-world lessons learned and best practices directly from peers who have gone through the journey. The opening sessions consistently highlight the critical role User Groups play in influencing SAP's roadmap and commercial decisions, demonstrating a genuine commitment to customer-driven innovation.

Comprehensive Coverage of Cloud ERP Strategy

Each SAP Transformation Day provides participants with a complete understanding of:

  • SAP Cloud ERP Strategy within the broader SAP Business Suite context
  • Portfolio Overview of Cloud ERP offerings including key deployment options (Private and Public Cloud) and solution highlights
  • Functional Differentiation between SAP Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise
  • Transformation and Migration Approaches tailored to different organizational needs
  • Customer Experience
  • Maintenance Strategy and Timelines for informed decision-making
  • Special Programs including Safekeeper, ERP Transition Options, and Compatibility Scope
  • Customer Experience: Information and feedback sharing from existing SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition customers

These events are designed to equip participants with:

  • Strategic insights into SAP's Cloud ERP vision
  • Practical guidance on transformation approaches (Brownfield vs. Greenfield)
  • Best practices from successful customer journeys
  • Understanding of AI capabilities and business value
  • Clear timelines and maintenance strategies
  • Change management considerations for sustainable adoption

The Power of Real Customer Experiences

One of the most valuable aspects of SAP Transformation Day is the inclusion of customer stories and first-hand experiences. These sessions go beyond theoretical frameworks to share:

  • Decision criteria for different transformation approaches
  • Key value drivers that made the difference
  • Practical lessons learned
  • Honest discussions about what works well and what challenges to anticipate

Looking Ahead

As SAP Transformation Day series continues to expand to new regions and formats, including the integration with major SAP events like SAPPHIRE, the series reinforces SAP's commitment to transparent communication and customer enablement.

The success of reaching over 1,100 customers across several regions demonstrates the strong demand for clear, comprehensive guidance on Cloud ERP transformation. More importantly, it showcases the power of collaboration between SAP and its User Group community in creating truly valuable customer experiences.

Join the Journey

Whether you're just beginning to explore SAP Cloud ERP options or are actively planning your transformation, SAP Transformation Day offers the strategic insights, practical guidance, and community connections you need to succeed.

Stay tuned for announcements about upcoming events in your region, and join the growing community of organizations confidently moving forward on their Cloud ERP transformation journey.

For more information about upcoming SAP Transformation Day events or to learn more about SAP Cloud ERP, connect with your local SAP User Group or contact your SAP representative.
